research.interface: ARM REST API (management.azure.com) with Microsoft Entra OAuth 2.0 v2 client-credentials flow; asynchronous-operation tracking per the ARM async-operations contract

official_sources.title: Track asynchronous Azure operations - Azure Resource Manager
official_sources.used_for: 201/202 initial status codes, Azure-AsyncOperation preferred over Location for the status monitor URL, Retry-After as seconds between polls, status monitor body with status Succeeded/Failed/Canceled plus error{code,message} on failure, provisioningState terminal values, Location-URL monitors answering 202 while running and 200 with the final body when done, and the 4 KB minimum monitor-URL size

official_sources.title: Azure REST API reference documentation
official_sources.used_for: management.azure.com base host, the mandatory api-version query parameter on ARM requests, the Authorization Bearer header, Content-Type application/json for request bodies, and the async/throttling/paging pointers

official_sources.title: OAuth 2.0 client credentials flow on the Microsoft identity platform
official_sources.used_for: token endpoint https://login.microsoftonline.com/{tenant}/oauth2/v2.0/token, application/x-www-form-urlencoded POST with grant_type=client_credentials, client_id, client_secret, and scope={resource}/.default, the token_type/expires_in/access_token success body, and the error/error_description failure body

official_sources.title: Microsoft Azure REST API Guidelines
official_sources.used_for: the Operation-Location status-monitor header used by newer Azure services, retry-after expressed as an integer number of seconds, terminal status values Succeeded/Failed/Canceled, and the error body rule that error.code matches the x-ms-error-code header

research.language_rationale: The ARM long-running-operation contract is defined at the REST level (response headers and status-monitor JSON), and Azure's own .NET SDK implements exactly this header state machine inside Azure.Core. Building the poller on System.Net.Http.HttpClient from the BCL is the documented raw integration surface, keeps the seed dependency-free, and makes the researched precedence/Retry-After rules the actual deliverable instead of hiding them behind an SDK poller.

verified_facts: An asynchronous ARM operation initially returns 201 (Created) or 202 (Accepted); 200 or 204 mean it completed synchronously.

Azure-AsyncOperation is the preferred status-monitor header; Location is used only when Azure-AsyncOperation is not returned. Newer Azure services expose the same monitor through Operation-Location, which therefore ranks between the two for a generic client.
Retry-After is an integer number of seconds the client should wait before checking the monitor again; when it is missing the client supplies its own retry policy.

The Azure-AsyncOperation monitor returns a JSON body whose required property is status; Succeeded, Failed, and Canceled are terminal, and any other value (for example InProgress or a provider-specific string) means the operation is still running.
The monitor body's error object (code, message) is present when status is Failed or Canceled.

A Location-style monitor answers 202 while the operation is running and 200 with the final payload when it is complete, unlike an Azure-AsyncOperation monitor which always answers 200 with a status document.
ARM requests go to https://management.azure.com, require an api-version query parameter, and authenticate with an Authorization: Bearer header; PUT bodies are application/json.

Client-credentials tokens come from POST https://login.microsoftonline.com/{tenant}/oauth2/v2.0/token as application/x-www-form-urlencoded with grant_type=client_credentials, client_id, client_secret, and scope set to the resource identifier suffixed with /.default (for ARM: https://management.azure.com/.default); the response carries token_type, expires_in (seconds), and access_token.
ARM error bodies carry a top-level error object with code and message; the code matches the x-ms-error-code header when present.
Clients must accept monitor URLs of at least 4 KB for Azure-AsyncOperation and Location.
